messages: Hi — I'm preparing for a job interview and could use some structured help.
It's a technical role and I want to be ready for both coding and behavioral parts.
I'd like mock questions, feedback on answers, and a realistic day-by-day practice plan.
Before I give details, what are the common pitfalls people miss when prepping for engineering screens?
How do you balance practicing algorithms versus system design and practical backend skills?
I get nervous during live coding and sometimes lose my train of thought — any general tips to stay focused?
What's an efficient way to practice whiteboard-style problems if I only have short blocks of time?
For behavioral interviews, do you recommend rehearsing full answers or practicing frameworks like STAR?
I want to sharpen a 30–60 second personal pitch — what structure usually works?
Also, what should I have ready for a virtual interview (repo links, slides, terminal setup, etc.)?
How can I get concrete feedback on my answers instead of vague 'good' or 'needs work'?
One more: how can I simulate the pacing and pressure of a real interview while practicing alone?
A bit more context: the company is a small fintech startup I applied to.
Given that, would you recommend emphasizing product impact and cross-functional ownership in my examples?
About my background: I have four years doing backend work, primarily writing Python and Django services that I've shipped to the cloud.
So when you tailor practice prompts, make them realistic for someone with that sort of production experience.
The recruiter told me the initial technical screen will be 45 minutes and remote; a successful screen could lead to an on-site day.
For that kind of first-round slot, how should I pace my answers between implementation detail and high-level trade-offs?
Another practical thing: I only have two weeks until that scheduled screen.
With such limited time, how would you prioritize topics across those fourteen days?
Also, I'm located in Central Europe and can realistically practice in the mornings before my day job and in the early evenings.
Could you adapt a study schedule to fit short morning sessions and a couple of longer evening blocks?
The job description specifically called out database performance and SQL querying, so I should be ready for DB-focused questions.
What are the highest-impact database concepts and query techniques to drill for interviews?
They also mentioned there might be a short take-home project with about a 48-hour turnaround and then a brief presentation.
If that happens, what structure makes the most sense for the deliverable and the live walkthrough?
One more disclosure: English isn't my first language, so I sometimes search for the right wording when I'm nervous.
Any concrete strategies to keep explanations clear and concise despite occasional word-finding under pressure?
Thanks — that's helpful. Can we now draft some concrete items I can use in mock sessions?
First, please draft a 30–60 second self-introduction I could use at the outset of a screen that highlights experience and impact.
Next, give me three short coding prompts suitable for a quick remote screen, each with a brief ideal approach and one-line hints.
Then provide three database/querying exercises that let me demonstrate performance-aware thinking, plus key talking points for each.
I'd also like two compact system-design prompts appropriate for a mid-level backend engineer, with bullet points on how to structure a concise answer.
Please write three behavioral prompts tailored to small, product-oriented companies and concise STAR-style bullet outlines for each.
Give me a slide-by-slide template for presenting a short take-home: what each slide should contain and what to say on each.
Because my practice time is fragmented, sketch a daily micro-plan that fits brief morning sessions and limited evening slots across the prep window.
Also, can you suggest quick drills and phrasing tips to help me speak more clearly when explaining technical choices under stress?
For the initial remote screen specifically, suggest an ideal agenda breakdown (how much time for coding, explanation, and Q&A) and what success looks like.
Could you also give a short checklist of artifacts and links I should have open or ready during a virtual interview?

Now please produce a single, actionable interview-prep package that pulls together everything I've mentioned: a tailored short pitch, mock technical and database prompts with model answers and hints, system-design and behavioral outlines, a slide-and-script template for presenting a short take-home, a day-by-day micro-practice schedule that fits my fragmented availability, and practical communication techniques to stay clear under pressure; make it realistic for the upcoming screen and the potential on-site sequence.
